It handles invoices, estimates, recurring billing, expense tracking, multi-currency support, and client and payment management without subscription costs. Official development slowed after 2021, and the community-maintained fork InvoiceShelf now continues the codebase with active updates.

enrichment.seo_title: Crater Invoicing Software: Free Open-Source Invoice App
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Crater is a free, open-source, self-hosted invoicing app for freelancers and small businesses. See features, pricing, and how it compares to InvoiceShelf.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Crater is a free, open-source invoicing and billing application originally released around 2019 by developers publishing under the crater-invoice-inc organization on GitHub, built using the Laravel PHP framework for its backend and Vue.js for its frontend, with companion mobile apps built in React Native. It is licensed under the GNU Affero General Public License version 3 (AGPLv3), meaning the source code is freely available to download, self-host, modify, and redistribute, provided that any hosted modifications are also made available under the same license. Crater is designed primarily for freelancers, consultants, and small businesses that want an invoicing tool without recurring software-as-a-service fees, as long as they are willing to install and maintain it themselves on their own server, a VPS, or a self-hosting platform such as Cloudron. Core functionality includes creating and sending professional invoices and estimates, converting estimates into invoices, setting up recurring or subscription-style invoices, recording payments against invoices, tracking business expenses, managing a client database, applying taxes and multiple currencies, and generating basic financial reports. The application supports PDF generation for invoices and estimates, customizable invoice templates, and a dashboard summarizing outstanding, paid, and overdue invoices. Because Crater is self-hosted, users are fully responsible for their own data, backups, security patching, and server costs, which distinguishes it from cloud invoicing tools like Zoho Invoice, Wave, or FreshBooks that host data on the vendor's infrastructure in exchange for a subscription fee. A significant fact for anyone evaluating Crater in 2026 is that its original repository entered a largely unsupported state after 2021, with infrequent commits and a backlog of open issues. In response, community members created InvoiceShelf, a hard fork of the Crater codebase, which has become the actively maintained continuation of the project, adding bug fixes, new features, and ongoing compatibility updates. Anyone deploying Crater fresh today is generally directed by the community to install InvoiceShelf instead, since it uses the same underlying data model and user interface. There is also an unrelated, differently branded commercial product called Crater Financial offering AI-powered accounts receivable and accounts payable automation, which should not be confused with the open-source Crater invoicing app covered here, since the two share a name but are separate products from separate teams. For SEO and directory purposes, the open-source Crater invoicing application is the entity most commonly referenced when people search for Crater invoicing or Crater app, and it remains a commonly cited example of self-hosted, no-subscription invoicing software in comparison articles and open-source software directories such as AlternativeTo and SourceForge. Because there is no central vendor, there is no traditional customer support, sales team, headquarters, or disclosed company size; support instead comes from GitHub issues, community documentation at docs.craterapp.com, and increasingly, the InvoiceShelf community and documentation. Pricing is inherently free at the software license level; the only costs a user incurs are for hosting infrastructure (a low-cost VPS is typically sufficient) and their own time to install and maintain the application, typically via Docker, a LAMP or LEMP stack, or a one-click installer on platforms like Cloudron or CapRover.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Crater
paragraphs: Crater is an open-source invoicing and billing web application that lets freelancers and small business owners create invoices, estimates, and recurring bills, track payments and expenses, and manage clients from a self-hosted dashboard. It was built on the Laravel PHP framework with a Vue.js frontend, and companion mobile apps were built in React Native for iOS and Android.

Because Crater is released under the AGPLv3 open-source license, anyone can download the source code from GitHub, install it on their own server, and use it indefinitely at no licensing cost. This makes it popular with developers, agencies, and cost-conscious small businesses that already have the technical know-how to run a self-hosted application, or who use one-click installers on platforms such as Cloudron.

long_form_blocks.heading: Crater vs. InvoiceShelf: Which One to Use
paragraphs: The original Crater repository (crater-invoice-inc/crater) saw a substantial drop in maintenance activity after 2021. To keep the project alive, a group of contributors created InvoiceShelf, a direct fork of Crater's codebase that has continued to receive bug fixes, security updates, and new features.

For new installations in 2026, the open-source community generally recommends InvoiceShelf over the original Crater repository, since it shares the same core invoicing, estimate, expense, and client-management features but is actively maintained. Existing Crater installations can typically migrate to InvoiceShelf because the underlying data structures are closely related.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Crater Is Built For
paragraphs: Crater (and its InvoiceShelf continuation) is best suited to freelancers, consultants, and small businesses that want full ownership of their invoicing data, are comfortable with basic server administration or a managed self-hosting platform, and want to avoid the recurring per-seat or per-invoice fees charged by cloud invoicing SaaS products.

It is less suited to non-technical users who need zero-setup, fully managed software, or to larger finance teams that require built-in payment gateway integrations, payroll, or advanced accounting features beyond invoicing, estimates, and basic expense tracking.

faqs.question: Is Crater really free?
faqs.answer: Yes. Crater is released under the AGPLv3 open-source license, so the software itself costs nothing; the only expense is the hosting infrastructure you run it on.

faqs.question: Is Crater still actively maintained?
faqs.answer: The original crater-invoice-inc repository has seen little activity since 2021. Active development now happens primarily through InvoiceShelf, a community fork built on the same codebase.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Crater and InvoiceShelf?
faqs.answer: InvoiceShelf is a fork of Crater created to continue development after the original project slowed down. It shares the same core invoicing features but receives ongoing updates and bug fixes.

faqs.question: Can I self-host Crater without technical experience?
faqs.answer: Some self-hosting platforms, such as Cloudron, offer one-click installers that simplify setup, but running Crater still requires basic server administration knowledge.

faqs.question: Does Crater support recurring billing?
faqs.answer: Yes, Crater supports recurring invoices for clients who are billed on a regular schedule, such as retainers or subscriptions.

faqs.question: Does Crater integrate with payment gateways?
faqs.answer: Crater includes basic payment recording, though its built-in online payment gateway integrations are more limited than many commercial cloud invoicing platforms.

faqs.question: Is Crater the same as Crater Financial?
faqs.answer: No. Crater Financial is a separate, unrelated commercial product focused on AI-powered accounts receivable and payable automation, distinct from the open-source Crater invoicing application.

faqs.question: Can Crater handle multiple currencies and taxes?
faqs.answer: Yes, Crater supports multi-currency invoicing and configurable tax rates.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Creately is a visual collaboration platform for flowcharts, mind maps, and diagrams. Compare pricing, features, pros and cons for teams in 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Creately is a visual collaboration and diagramming software platform built by Cinergix Pty Ltd, a company founded in 2008 and headquartered in Melbourne, Australia, with its primary engineering and support team based in Colombo, Sri Lanka. The product lets individuals and teams create flowcharts, mind maps, organizational charts, process diagrams, genograms, wireframes, and general whiteboard-style visuals on an infinite online canvas, with real-time multi-user editing so multiple collaborators can work on the same diagram simultaneously and see each other's changes instantly. Creately supports more than 70 distinct diagram types and ships with a library of over 10,000 pre-built templates spanning business strategy, product management, software architecture, education, and personal planning use cases. A differentiating feature is its Smart Canvas and Visual Database approach, which lets users attach structured data, notes, and file attachments directly to shapes and diagrams, effectively turning a diagram into a lightweight database that stays synchronized as underlying data changes; this is paired with two-way sync to spreadsheets such as Excel and Google Sheets on higher-tier plans. Creately has also added AI-powered diagramming tools, marketed under the VIZ AI name, that can generate flowcharts, mind maps, or diagram layouts from typed text prompts, suggest diagram structure, and help clean up or reorganize existing diagrams, reducing the manual work of building a diagram shape by shape. As of 2026, Creately's pricing follows a freemium model: a permanently free plan offers unlimited canvases and collaborators but caps each canvas at 45 items and includes only standard shape libraries; a Starter plan, billed monthly or with an annual discount, raises the per-canvas item limit to 1,000 and adds premium templates, AI diagramming, unlimited imports, and export to JPEG, PNG, SVG, and PDF; a Team plan, billed per user per month, adds a higher item cap, advanced collaboration controls, and account management tools for growing teams; a Business plan, billed as a flat monthly rate, adds Microsoft Teams integration, two-way Excel and Google Sheets sync, a database feature for up to 50 linked databases, one year of version history, project management tools, and a dedicated customer success manager; and an Enterprise plan offers custom pricing with unlimited items per canvas, custom data residency, white-glove onboarding, and on-premise deployment options for large or regulated organizations. Creately is commonly used by product and engineering teams for process mapping and system diagrams, by operations and consulting teams for org charts and workflow documentation, by educators and students for concept maps and mind maps, and by project managers for planning boards and roadmaps. It competes most directly with Lucidchart, Miro, diagrams.net, and Visio in the broader diagramming and visual collaboration category, differentiating itself through its combination of structured data linking, a large template library, and AI-assisted diagram generation at a lower price point than some enterprise-focused competitors. Because Creately is a subscription SaaS product rather than open source or self-hosted software, all diagrams and account data are stored in Creately's cloud infrastructure, and enterprise customers requiring on-premise or data-residency controls need the custom Enterprise tier. The company has grown from a small Melbourne-founded startup into a globally distributed team serving individual professionals, small teams, and larger enterprise accounts, with reviews and ratings available on sites such as G2, Capterra, and Gartner Peer Insights.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Creately
paragraphs: Creately is a cloud-based visual collaboration and diagramming platform that lets teams build flowcharts, mind maps, org charts, wireframes, and process diagrams on a shared, infinite canvas. It was created by Cinergix, a company founded in 2008 with headquarters in Melbourne, Australia and its core team based in Colombo, Sri Lanka.

The platform supports real-time collaboration, meaning multiple people can edit the same diagram at once and see live cursor movement and updates, along with commenting, notes, and version history for reviewing how a diagram evolved over time.

long_form_blocks.heading: AI and Smart Canvas Features
paragraphs: Creately's AI tools, marketed as VIZ AI, can generate a starting diagram or mind map from a typed text prompt, suggest layout improvements, and help reorganize cluttered diagrams, cutting down the manual effort of building complex flowcharts from scratch.

Its Smart Canvas and Visual Database features let users attach structured data fields, files, and notes directly to diagram shapes, and on higher-tier plans this data can sync two-way with Excel or Google Sheets, effectively turning a static diagram into a live, data-connected view of a project or process.

long_form_blocks.heading: How Creately Pricing Works
paragraphs: Creately uses a freemium pricing structure. The Free plan supports unlimited canvases and collaborators but limits each canvas to 45 items and standard shape libraries, making it suitable for light personal use or evaluation.

Paid tiers (Starter, Team, and Business) progressively raise per-canvas item limits and add premium templates, AI diagramming, integrations, and administrative controls, while the custom Enterprise plan adds unlimited items, on-premise deployment, and dedicated support for large organizations.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Creatify turns product links into AI-generated video ads with avatars and voiceovers. See 2026 pricing, features, pros, cons, and use cases.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Creatify is an AI video ad generation platform founded in 2023 by former Meta and Snap technologists, including contributors to Meta's FAIR research lab, and is headquartered in the San Francisco Bay Area. The core product lets marketers generate short-form marketing and advertising videos automatically from a product URL, a written product description, or existing footage, without needing a camera crew, professional actors, or video editing skills. Users can select from a library of AI avatars (Creatify offers hundreds to well over a thousand AI actors depending on plan tier, plus the ability to create custom avatars on higher tiers), choose from hundreds of ad templates, and generate scripts, voiceovers, and captions automatically in more than 75 languages, which makes the platform popular for localizing the same ad concept across multiple international markets. Beyond generation, Creatify has expanded into what it calls an end-to-end AI ad agent for video, adding features such as an AI Media Buyer that can help manage ad spend, a competitor ad tracker for monitoring rival brands' creative, and a built-in ad launcher that pushes finished videos directly to Meta, TikTok, and AppLovin ad accounts, reducing the number of separate tools a performance marketing team needs. As of 2026, Creatify's pricing is credit-based: a free plan includes 10 monthly credits with watermarked exports for testing the product; the Starter plan costs 39 dollars per month and includes 100 credits per month, access to roughly 300 AI actors, 200-plus ad templates, and over 50 premium models, along with watermark removal and support for 75-plus languages; the Pro plan, the most popular tier, costs 99 dollars per month for 300 credits per month, scalable up to 5,000, roughly 1,500 AI actors plus 3 custom avatar seats, 500-plus templates, over 100 premium models, the AI Media Buyer, the competitor ad tracker, the multi-platform ad launcher, and team collaboration for up to 5 seats; and an Enterprise plan offers custom pricing with a larger annual credit allotment, a done-for-you Creatify Studio service, AI-driven ad performance guidance, more seats or brand spaces, API volume discounts, custom templates, white-label options, and a dedicated account manager. Annual billing on paid plans offers savings of up to roughly 50 percent compared to monthly billing. Each video render consumes a variable number of credits depending on video length, whether an AI avatar is used, and which underlying AI model generates the content, so actual cost per finished video varies by use case. Creatify has attracted institutional backing, closing a 15.5 million dollar Series A funding round in 2025 co-led by WndrCo and Kindred Ventures, bringing total funding to roughly 23 million dollars, with early investors including NFDG, Millennium New Horizons, Creator Ventures, and Leadout Capital; DreamWorks Animation co-founder and WndrCo founding partner Jeffrey Katzenberg joined the company's board as part of the round. The company reports crossing 9 million dollars in annual recurring revenue around the time of that raise and states its platform is used by more than a million marketers and over 10,000 teams, including brands such as Alibaba.com, Comcast, Binance, NewsBreak, Tec-do, and Zumper. Creatify competes in the AI video ad generation category alongside tools such as Arcads, Icon, and Pictory, and is most commonly adopted by e-commerce brands, direct-response performance marketing agencies, and in-house growth marketing teams that need to produce and test many video ad variations quickly and cost-effectively.

long_form_blocks.heading: How Creatify Generates Video Ads
paragraphs: Creatify converts a product URL, uploaded product images, or a written description into a finished marketing video by combining an AI-generated or user-selected script, an AI avatar or voiceover, background footage or product shots, and a chosen ad template. The result is a short-form video styled for platforms like TikTok, Instagram Reels, YouTube Shorts, and Meta ad placements.

Because scripts and voiceovers can be generated in more than 75 languages, brands use Creatify to localize the same underlying ad concept for multiple international markets without re-shooting footage or hiring local voice talent for every language.

long_form_blocks.heading: From Video Generator to AI Ad Agent
paragraphs: Beyond generating individual videos, Creatify has added tools aimed at running the full paid social workflow: an AI Media Buyer to help manage and optimize ad spend, a competitor ad tracker to monitor what rival brands are running, and a native ad launcher that publishes finished videos directly to Meta, TikTok, and AppLovin ad accounts.

This positions Creatify as more than a creative-generation tool; the company markets it as an end-to-end AI ad agent for video, aiming to reduce the number of separate platforms a performance marketing team needs for ideation, production, and launch.

long_form_blocks.heading: Company Background and Funding
paragraphs: Creatify was founded in 2023 by former Meta and Snap technologists, some of whom contributed to Meta's FAIR research lab, and is based in the San Francisco Bay Area. The company closed a 15.5 million dollar Series A funding round in 2025, co-led by WndrCo and Kindred Ventures, bringing total funding to roughly 23 million dollars.

DreamWorks Animation co-founder Jeffrey Katzenberg, a founding partner at WndrCo, joined Creatify's board as part of the Series A. The company has reported crossing 9 million dollars in annual recurring revenue and states its tools are used by more than a million marketers, including brands such as Alibaba.com, Comcast, and Binance.

This entry is written as an honest, clearly labeled overview of the broader category that the phrase most plausibly refers to, rather than as a profile of one confirmed vendor, and readers evaluating a specific tool under this name should independently verify the exact product and company before drawing conclusions from this page. In financial services and fintech contexts, credit mapping commonly describes activities such as mapping a borrower's financial statements, tax returns, or credit bureau data into a structured chart of accounts or risk model, so that lenders and credit teams can calculate ratios, spread financials, and assess creditworthiness consistently across a portfolio; it can also refer to visually mapping the relationships, guarantors, and corporate hierarchy connected to a borrower for risk assessment. Software that performs functions consistent with credit mapping generally falls under the umbrella of credit risk management, credit decisioning, and financial spreading software. Established vendors serving this broader category include FIS, through its Credit Assessment and Commercial Lending Suite products offering configurable financial spreading and risk rating; C and R Software's FitLogic, a visual, rules-based credit decisioning configuration tool aimed at bridging business and technical teams; Experian's credit decisioning tools; Billtrust's AI-assisted accounts-receivable credit management software; HighRadius's AI-driven credit and order-to-cash systems; and newer AI-native entrants such as Hebbia, which automates the extraction and mapping of financial statement data into a chart of accounts for faster credit analysis. These platforms are generally sold to banks, commercial lenders, credit unions, business-to-business finance and accounts-receivable teams, and corporate credit departments, typically through enterprise sales processes with custom, quote-based pricing rather than public self-serve pricing pages, reflecting the complexity and compliance requirements of commercial lending and credit risk workflows. Because no single, verifiable Credit Mapping product was identified, this profile intentionally avoids inventing details such as a founding year, headquarters, company size, or specific pricing plans for a named company, since any of those specifics would be fabricated without a confirmed source. If a listing for credit mapping on gappsy.com is intended to reference a specific, differently branded product, that product should be re-verified by name and this entry updated accordingly, since credit mapping as researched here reflects a functional category description within credit risk and lending software rather than a single company's offering. Prospective buyers researching this space in 2026 should treat credit mapping as a search term or functional description they can use to find relevant financial spreading, credit decisioning, and credit risk management vendors, evaluating each candidate vendor's specific capabilities, integrations with credit bureaus and accounting systems, compliance certifications, and pricing directly with that vendor rather than assuming a single unified product exists under this name.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Credit Mapping Actually Refers To
paragraphs: No single, verifiable software product publicly known by the exact name Credit Mapping was identified through current research. It can also describe visually mapping relationships between a borrower, guarantors, and related corporate entities for risk and exposure assessment. Both usages fall under the broader financial spreading and credit risk management software category rather than naming one specific application.

long_form_blocks.heading: Vendors Serving the Credit Mapping and Financial Spreading Category

Other relevant vendors include Experian's credit decisioning tools, Billtrust's AI-assisted credit management software for accounts receivable teams, HighRadius's AI-driven credit and order-to-cash platform, and Hebbia, an AI tool that automates extracting and mapping financial statement data into a chart of accounts for credit analysis.

enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Cricket Health was a venture-backed, technology-enabled kidney care company founded in 2014 by former LinkedIn executive Arvind Rajan and former Aberdare Ventures general partner Vince Kim, and it publicly launched its value-based chronic kidney disease (CKD) care model in 2016. The company was headquartered in San Francisco, California, and built a technology and patient engagement platform designed to identify at-risk kidney disease patients earlier, support them through education and multidisciplinary care teams, and help them make more informed treatment decisions, including planned rather than emergency starts to dialysis and increased use of home dialysis and kidney transplant options. Cricket Health's business model was built around value-based care contracts with health plans and provider groups, meaning it was paid based on improving patient outcomes and reducing the total cost of care for chronic kidney disease and end-stage renal disease populations, rather than selling software licenses or consumer subscriptions directly. The company raised early venture funding, including a 2.5 million dollar seed round led by First Round Capital, BoxGroup, Seven Peaks Ventures, Nexus Venture Partners, and NFX Guild, and later funding rounds including roughly 24 million dollars raised by 2018 to expand its footprint in the kidney care space. The most important fact for anyone researching Cricket Health in 2026 is that it no longer exists as an independent, separately branded company. In August 2022, Cricket Health completed a three-way merger with InterWell Health and Fresenius Health Partners, a division of Fresenius Medical Care North America, in a transaction reported to be valued at approximately 2.4 billion dollars. The combined organization retained the InterWell Health name rather than the Cricket Health name, and Cricket Health's technology-enabled care model and patient engagement platform were integrated into InterWell Health's broader value-based kidney care operations. InterWell Health is now headquartered at 920 Winter Street, Waltham, Massachusetts, and operates as one of the largest value-based kidney care providers in the United States, working with a large network of nephrologists, historically cited at more than 1,600, and aiming to serve several hundred thousand patients with chronic kidney disease and end-stage renal disease. Because Cricket Health has been absorbed into InterWell Health, there is no longer a standalone Cricket Health product, pricing page, or company website functioning independently; searches for Cricket Health in 2026 should be understood as referring to a discontinued brand whose capabilities live on inside InterWell Health. Any directory listing, comparison, or review referencing Cricket Health as an active, separately operating company would be inaccurate as of 2026, and this entry is written to reflect that historical and current status honestly rather than describing Cricket Health as if it were still a currently operating standalone product. For readers actually looking for the active kidney care organization that resulted from this merger, InterWell Health is the correct current entity to research, as it now carries forward Cricket Health's technology-enabled care approach alongside Fresenius Health Partners' value-based contracting expertise and InterWell Health's nephrology network.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Cricket Health Was
paragraphs: Cricket Health was a San Francisco-based, technology-enabled kidney care company founded in 2014 that publicly launched its value-based chronic kidney disease care model in 2016. It combined a patient engagement platform with multidisciplinary clinical teams to help patients with chronic kidney disease and end-stage renal disease get earlier education, better treatment planning, and more access to home dialysis and transplant options.

Rather than selling software directly to consumers, Cricket Health contracted with health plans and provider groups under value-based care arrangements, getting paid in part based on improving patient outcomes and lowering the total cost of caring for kidney disease populations.

long_form_blocks.heading: The 2022 Merger Into InterWell Health
paragraphs: In August 2022, Cricket Health completed a three-way merger with InterWell Health and Fresenius Health Partners, a division of Fresenius Medical Care North America. The deal was reported to be valued at approximately 2.4 billion dollars and combined Fresenius Health Partners' value-based contracting expertise, InterWell Health's large nephrology network, and Cricket Health's technology and patient engagement platform.

The combined company retained the InterWell Health name rather than continuing to operate as Cricket Health, and the merger was positioned as creating one of the nation's premier value-based kidney care providers, aiming to serve close to 300,000 patients and manage roughly 11 billion dollars in medical costs.

long_form_blocks.heading: Cricket Health's Status in 2026
paragraphs: As of 2026, Cricket Health does not exist as an independent, separately branded company. Its technology and care model live on inside InterWell Health, which is now headquartered in Waltham, Massachusetts.

Anyone researching Cricket Health today should treat it as a discontinued standalone brand and look to InterWell Health for the current, active organization and its kidney care programs, technology platform, and provider network.

enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Criteo is a publicly traded advertising technology company, listed on NASDAQ under the ticker CRTO, that operates what it calls a commerce media platform connecting advertisers, retailers, and media owners around commerce-focused digital advertising. The company was founded in Paris, France in 2005 by Jean-Baptiste Rudelle, Franck Le Ouay, and Romain Niccoli, and it built its early reputation and much of its revenue on dynamic retargeting: showing personalized product ads to shoppers who had previously viewed items on a retailer's website but had not completed a purchase, using machine learning to predict which products and creative were most likely to drive a click and a sale. Criteo went public on NASDAQ in 2013 and remains headquartered in Paris, with a large global employee base reported at roughly 3,300 to 3,900 people depending on the reporting period, and offices across North America, Europe, and Asia-Pacific. Over time Criteo has broadened well beyond retargeting into a fuller commerce media and retail media business, rebranding its overall offering as the Commerce Media Platform. This platform includes a Commerce Media demand-side platform used by enterprise brands, retailers, and agencies to plan and buy commerce-focused advertising across a claimed network of more than 200 retailers and over 1,300 premium publishers; a next-generation supply-side platform, marketed as Commerce Grid, that lets media owners, agencies, and retailers connect their inventory and first-party commerce data to programmatic demand; and a Monetization Platform aimed at helping large retailers and marketplaces build and scale their own retail media networks, similar to what Amazon Ads or Walmart Connect operate internally. Criteo's pricing model is performance-based rather than a flat-rate software subscription: advertisers typically set daily or lifetime budgets and pay on a cost-per-click or cost-per-impression basis, with Criteo layering a platform fee calculated as a percentage of media spend on top of the underlying media cost; industry estimates put Criteo's effective revenue share in the rough range of 16 to 22 percent of ad spend, though exact rates vary by client, campaign type, and volume, and larger accounts can access managed-service arrangements alongside newer pricing levers such as first-party data onboarding fees and tiered self-serve pricing. Criteo's core technical differentiator has long been its access to large volumes of first-party commerce and shopper behavior data from retail partners, combined with AI and machine learning models used for real-time bidding, product recommendation, and audience targeting, which the company positions as an alternative or complement to advertising on Google, Meta, and Amazon. Because Criteo is a large, established public company rather than an early-stage startup, its most relevant facts for buyers and researchers in 2026 are its retail media and commerce media platform positioning, its scale across thousands of advertiser and publisher relationships, its NASDAQ listing and quarterly earnings disclosures, and its ongoing evolution from a pure retargeting vendor into a broader commerce media infrastructure provider competing with retail media networks and other adtech platforms across the growing retail media advertising category.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Criteo Does
paragraphs: Criteo is a publicly traded (NASDAQ: CRTO) advertising technology company founded in Paris in 2005, originally known for dynamic retargeting: showing shoppers personalized product ads based on items they viewed on a retailer's site but did not purchase. Its machine learning models predict which products, creative, and placements are most likely to drive a click and eventual sale.

The company has since expanded into the broader commerce media and retail media category, connecting advertisers, retailers, and publishers around advertising informed by first-party commerce and shopper data rather than only browser-based retargeting.

long_form_blocks.heading: The Commerce Media Platform
paragraphs: Criteo's current offering is branded as the Commerce Media Platform, made up of a demand-side platform for brands, retailers, and agencies to plan and buy commerce media across a large network of retailer and publisher partners; a supply-side platform called Commerce Grid for media owners and retailers to monetize their inventory programmatically; and a Monetization Platform that helps large retailers build their own retail media networks.

This shift reflects the broader retail media industry trend of retailers and marketplaces, following the model popularized by Amazon Ads, turning their own shopper data and digital real estate into advertising revenue, with Criteo positioning itself as an infrastructure and technology partner for that trend rather than only a retargeting vendor.

long_form_blocks.heading: How Criteo's Pricing and Fees Work
paragraphs: Advertisers using Criteo generally pay on a cost-per-click or cost-per-impression basis against budgets they set themselves, rather than a fixed monthly software fee. Criteo adds a platform fee calculated as a percentage of media spend, with industry estimates placing its effective take rate in the rough range of 16 to 22 percent, though actual rates depend on the client, campaign type, and volume.

Larger advertisers and retail media networks can also work with Criteo through managed-service arrangements, and the company has introduced additional pricing levers such as first-party data onboarding fees and tiered self-serve pricing as its business has diversified beyond classic retargeting.

enrichment.short_description: croc is a free, open-source command-line tool for securely transferring files and folders between two computers using a simple code phrase, without needing a local server, port forwarding, or shared cloud storage. It uses password-authenticated key exchange (PAKE) to establish an encryption key and then encrypts transfers with AES-256, relaying encrypted data through a relay server that cannot read the file contents. Created by developer Zack Scholl (schollz) and released on GitHub, croc supports Windows, macOS, and Linux, and can use the default public relay or a self-hosted one.

enrichment.seo_title: croc: Free Secure Command-Line File Transfer Tool
enrichment.seo_meta_description: croc is a free, open-source CLI tool for sending files securely between computers using PAKE encryption. See features, pros, cons, and FAQs.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: croc is a free, open-source command-line utility for securely and easily transferring files and folders between two computers, created by developer Zack Scholl, GitHub username schollz, and published as the schollz/croc repository. It is designed to solve a common developer and power-user problem: sending a file from one machine to another, potentially across different networks or behind NAT and firewalls, without setting up a local server, configuring port forwarding, or uploading the file to a third-party cloud storage service first. To use croc, a sender runs a single command to offer a file, folder, or piece of text, and croc generates a short, human-readable code phrase; the recipient runs croc with that code phrase on their own machine, and the transfer begins automatically once both sides connect. Security is handled through password-authenticated key exchange (PAKE), a cryptographic protocol that lets two parties who share a weak, human-readable secret (the code phrase) derive a strong, one-time session key without ever transmitting the code phrase itself over the network, protecting against eavesdropping even on the relay path. Once the session key is established, croc encrypts both the file data and its metadata using AES-256 before sending it, so that the connection between sender and receiver is end-to-end encrypted. Because most consumer and corporate networks block inbound connections and prevent two arbitrary computers from connecting to each other directly, croc routes the already encrypted data through an intermediary relay server; the relay's only job is to pass encrypted bytes back and forth, and because the data is encrypted before it reaches the relay, the relay operator cannot read the file contents. croc defaults to using a public relay server operated by the project, but it also supports self-hosting a private relay, including via Docker, which organizations often do when they have policy concerns about routing files through a third-party public relay, or when a public relay is unreachable inside a restricted network such as certain VPNs. Typical configuration uses TCP ports in the 9009 to 9013 range, though users can customize ports as long as at least two are open. croc supports resuming interrupted transfers, sending multiple files or entire folders in a single operation, cross-platform use across Windows, macOS, and Linux, and can optionally route traffic through a proxy such as Tor for additional privacy, alongside IPv6-first connectivity with automatic IPv4 fallback. Because croc is a command-line tool rather than a graphical application, it is most popular with developers, system administrators, and technically comfortable users who want a fast, scriptable, and secure alternative to email attachments, USB drives, or general-purpose cloud storage for one-off or repeated file transfers between machines they control. As free and open-source software hosted on GitHub, croc has no company, pricing plans, or customer support organization behind it; support comes from the GitHub repository's issues and documentation, and installation is typically done through package managers such as Homebrew, Scoop, or apt, or by downloading a prebuilt binary from the project's GitHub releases page.

long_form_blocks.heading: How croc Transfers Files Securely
paragraphs: croc lets a sender offer a file, folder, or text snippet from the command line, generating a short code phrase that the receiver enters on their own machine to start the transfer. Behind the scenes, croc uses password-authenticated key exchange (PAKE) so both sides derive a strong encryption key from that code phrase without ever sending the phrase itself over the network.

Once the key exchange completes, croc encrypts the file data and metadata with AES-256 before transmission, so the transfer is end-to-end encrypted between sender and receiver, even though the traffic passes through an intermediary relay server along the way.

long_form_blocks.heading: Relay Servers and Self-Hosting
paragraphs: Because most networks block direct inbound connections between two arbitrary computers, croc relays the already-encrypted data through a relay server that only passes bytes back and forth and cannot read the underlying file contents. By default, croc uses a public relay operated by the project.

Users and organizations that prefer not to route data through a public third-party relay, or that need croc to work inside restricted networks such as certain VPNs, can self-host their own relay, including through a simple Docker deployment, and configure croc to use it at runtime.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who croc Is Built For
paragraphs: croc is a command-line tool, so it is aimed primarily at developers, system administrators, and technically comfortable users who are comfortable working in a terminal. It fits well into scripted workflows, server-to-server transfers, and situations where installing a graphical file-sharing app is impractical.

It is less suited to non-technical users who want a graphical drag-and-drop interface, though its simple code-phrase-based workflow is still considerably easier to use than manually configuring SCP, FTP, or a local file server.

faqs.question: Is croc free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, croc is free and open-source software with no licensing fees.

faqs.question: How does croc keep file transfers secure?
faqs.answer: croc uses password-authenticated key exchange (PAKE) to derive a strong encryption key from a shared code phrase, then encrypts the file data and metadata with AES-256 before sending it.

faqs.question: Do I need to configure a server to use croc?
faqs.answer: No, croc uses a relay server, public by default or self-hosted, to route encrypted traffic between sender and receiver without requiring manual server setup or port forwarding in most cases.

faqs.question: Can the relay server read my files?
faqs.answer: No, because files are encrypted end-to-end before reaching the relay, the relay only passes encrypted bytes and cannot read the file contents.

faqs.question: What platforms does croc support?
faqs.answer: croc supports Windows, macOS, and Linux.

faqs.question: Can I self-host a croc relay?
faqs.answer: Yes, croc supports self-hosting a private relay server, including via Docker, for users or organizations that prefer not to use the public relay.

faqs.question: Can croc resume an interrupted transfer?
faqs.answer: Yes, croc supports resuming transfers that are interrupted partway through.

faqs.question: Who created croc?
faqs.answer: croc was created by developer Zack Scholl, published under the GitHub username schollz, as the open-source schollz/croc project.

Notion acquired Cron in 2022, and in January 2024 the standalone Cron app was retired and its technology relaunched as Notion Calendar, an integrated calendar product within the Notion ecosystem. enrichment.seo_title: Cron Calendar App: History, Notion Acquisition and Status
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Cron was a calendar app acquired by Notion in 2022 and relaunched as Notion Calendar in 2024. Learn its history, features, and current status.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Cron, also known by its domain cron.com, was a calendar application built for professionals and teams, founded in 2019 by Swiss-born software engineer and designer Raphael Schaad. The company went through Y Combinator's 2020 batch and raised a 3.5 million dollar seed round in April 2020 before launching its calendar app in public beta in 2021, where it was subsequently named Product Hunt's productivity app of the year for 2021. Cron was based in San Francisco, California, and built its reputation on a fast, keyboard-driven, visually polished calendar experience that connected to Google Calendar and, later, other calendar providers, offering features such as command-bar-style keyboard shortcuts, world clock and multiple time zone support, availability sharing and scheduling links, meeting templates, and tight integration with video conferencing tools, positioning itself as a modern successor to earlier well-regarded calendar apps like Sunrise Calendar. In June 2022, Notion Labs, Inc. announced it was acquiring Cron in a deal reported to be worth an eight-figure sum, with founder Raphael Schaad and his team joining Notion; at the time of the acquisition, Notion said Cron would continue to operate as a standalone app for a transition period. That transition period ended in January 2024, when Notion officially retired the separate Cron brand and relaunched the product as Notion Calendar, an integrated calendar application built directly on Cron's underlying technology and design but now marketed and distributed as part of the broader Notion productivity suite, including deep integration with Notion databases and documents so that calendar events can link to and pull information from a user's Notion workspace. Because of this history, it is important to distinguish the Cron calendar app covered in this profile from the completely unrelated Unix and Linux cron job scheduler, a decades-old system utility used to run scheduled command-line tasks; the two share only a name and have no connection to each other. As of 2026, the Cron calendar app referenced here, cron.com, is discontinued as an independently branded, independently distributed product. Existing Cron users were transitioned to Notion Calendar, and new users looking for Cron as a calendar app are, in practice, looking for Notion Calendar, which remains actively developed and is offered free of charge as part of the Notion ecosystem, available as a desktop app for macOS and Windows, a mobile app for iOS and Android, and a web version, with continued support for connecting multiple Google and Outlook calendar accounts. For SEO, directory, and comparison purposes in 2026, any reference to Cron as a currently available, separately branded calendar product would be inaccurate; the historically accurate and currently useful framing is that Cron was the predecessor product and brand, Notion acquired it in 2022, and Notion Calendar is its actively maintained continuation today.

long_form_blocks.heading: What the Cron Calendar App Was
paragraphs: Cron (cron.com) was a calendar application founded in 2019 by Raphael Schaad, built for professionals and teams who wanted a faster, more keyboard-driven, and more visually polished alternative to default calendar apps like Google Calendar or Apple Calendar. It went through Y Combinator's 2020 batch, raised a 3.5 million dollar seed round, and launched publicly in 2021, earning recognition as Product Hunt's productivity app of the year that year.

The app offered features such as keyboard shortcuts for quick navigation and scheduling, support for multiple time zones and a world clock view, availability sharing, meeting templates, and integration with video conferencing tools, and it connected to Google Calendar accounts, drawing comparisons to the earlier, well-loved Sunrise Calendar app.

long_form_blocks.heading: The Notion Acquisition and Rebrand to Notion Calendar
paragraphs: In June 2022, Notion Labs, Inc. announced its acquisition of Cron for a reported eight-figure sum, with founder Raphael Schaad and his team joining Notion while Cron initially continued operating as a standalone app.

In January 2024, Notion retired the separate Cron brand and relaunched the product as Notion Calendar, an integrated calendar app built on Cron's technology but now deeply connected to Notion's databases and documents, allowing calendar events to link directly to a user's broader Notion workspace.

long_form_blocks.heading: Cron the Calendar App vs. Unix cron
paragraphs: It is important not to confuse the Cron calendar app with the Unix and Linux cron job scheduler, a long-standing, unrelated system utility used to automatically run scheduled commands or scripts on a server. Despite the shared name, they have no corporate, technical, or historical connection.
This profile covers only the Cron calendar app, cron.com, founded by Raphael Schaad, its acquisition by Notion, and its continuation as Notion Calendar, not the Unix cron scheduling utility.

faqs.question: Is Cron still available as its own app?
faqs.answer: No. Cron was retired as a standalone, separately branded app in January 2024 after Notion acquired the company in 2022; its technology continues as Notion Calendar.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Cron and Notion Calendar?
faqs.answer: Notion Calendar is the direct successor to Cron, built on the same underlying technology after Notion's 2022 acquisition, but integrated into the broader Notion productivity suite with links to Notion pages and databases.

faqs.question: Who founded Cron?
faqs.answer: Cron was founded in 2019 by Raphael Schaad, a Swiss-born software engineer and designer, and the company went through Y Combinator's 2020 batch.

faqs.question: When did Notion acquire Cron?
faqs.answer: Notion announced its acquisition of Cron in June 2022, in a deal reported to be worth an eight-figure sum.

faqs.question: Is the Cron calendar app the same as the Unix cron command?
faqs.answer: No, they are completely unrelated. The Unix and Linux cron utility is a decades-old system tool for scheduling automated command-line tasks, while Cron, cron.com, was a consumer and team calendar application.

faqs.question: Can I still download the original Cron app?
faqs.answer: No, the standalone Cron app was retired in January 2024; users were transitioned to Notion Calendar, which continues to be actively developed.

faqs.question: Was Cron free or paid?
faqs.answer: As a standalone product, Cron's exact historical pricing details are not the focus of current documentation since it was retired; its successor, Notion Calendar, is offered free as part of the Notion ecosystem.

faqs.question: Where was Cron headquartered?
faqs.answer: Cron was based in San Francisco, California, prior to its 2022 acquisition by Notion.
